thread.title: TSSAA against ‘transfer portal’ but anticipates discussions with legislators to change transfer rule
thread.title_full: TSSAA against ‘transfer portal’ but anticipates discussions with legislators to change transfer rule

author: @WSMV
title: TSSAA against ‘transfer portal’ but anticipates discussions with legislators to change transfer rule
text:
TSSAA against ‘transfer portal’ but anticipates discussions with legislators to change transfer rule
The TSSAA’s executive director is adamant that its current transfer rules are the biggest deterrent to illegal recruiting.
NASHVILLE, Tenn. (WSMV) - The Tennessee Secondary School Athletic Association says that it anticipates discussions with lawmakers over the possibility of changing its transfer rule.
Executive Director Mark Reeves reports that member schools in the TSSAA were recently polled on a one-time transfer option.
The results were as follows:
- 78% of independent schools and 58% that participated in the voting were against a one-time transfer.
In a statement to WSMV4, Reeves expressed concerns that high school sports are much different from college athletics. Also in the statement, he was adamant that the TSSAA’s current transfer rules are the biggest deterrent to illegal recruiting.
“Although school athletic recruiting is a huge part of the culture of college athletics and is permissible, it is not permissible for school personnel at the middle school and high school levels. The transfer rules currently in place are the biggest deterrent to illegal recruiting. If a one-time transfer is given, it would stand to reason that we will see many more students recruited away from their schools for athletic purposes. This creates issues for the athletic programs at the school they are leaving and the school they are transferring to. In speaking with the Executive Director of another state with very few transfer restrictions, he indicated that their office had received numerous calls from families whose children had been a part of the school community for years and were suddenly displaced on a roster by transfer students seeking a better athletic team. In fact, they were dealing with one situation where a school had 32 students who had transferred to a single school in an effort to win a state championship in football. I need help to fathom how that could be in the best interest of students.”
Reeves also shared the TSSAA’s primary objectives they aim to achieve through its bylaws:
- Maintaining athletics in its proper perspective as subordinate to the primary academic mission of the schools;
- Preventing the exploitation of students for athletic purposes
- Fostering fair competition, or a “level playing field,” among the TSSAA member schools.
Finally, he added that a “transfer portal” in Tennessee middle and high schools would contradict the above objectives in many transfer situations.
“Yes, there are legitimate cases where a student transfers solely for an academic opportunity or specialized curriculum not offered at other schools,” Reeves said. “However, there would be a number of students transferring because they have a disagreement with a coach over playing time or just because the grass looks greener on the other side.”
Lastly, he said that some school communities that have had a hard time “keeping up with the Joneses” would lose students to other schools with nicer facilities or other things.
